question: What is the correct HTML5 doctype declaration?
correct: <!DOCTYPE html>

question: Which of the following correctly describes the difference between an HTML element and an HTML tag?
options: They are identical terms and can be used interchangeably
A tag is just the opening marker; an element includes the opening tag, content, and closing tag
An element is only the text content inside the tags
A tag includes both the opening and closing markers; an element is only the opening marker
correct: A tag is just the opening marker; an element includes the opening tag, content, and closing tag

question:
What will be the rendered output of:

Hello World

?
correct: Hello

question: Which attribute is mandatory for an element to be considered accessible?
correct: alt

question: In the HTML parent/child structure, which statement is correct about sibling elements?
options: Siblings share the same parent element
Siblings are elements nested inside each other
A sibling is always the first child of its parent
Siblings must be the same HTML tag type
correct: Siblings share the same parent element

question: What is the correct way to open a hyperlink in a new browser tab?
correct: <a href='url' target='_blank'>

question: Which HTML element should be used to represent a list where the order of items does NOT matter?
correct: <ul>

question:
Given this HTML:
  1. Item
  2. Item
, what numbers will the list items display?
Which is the correct HTML to create a table cell that spans 2 columns and 2 rows?
correct: <td colspan='2' rowspan='2'>

question: What is the purpose of the <caption> element inside a <table>?
options: It adds a tooltip to the table on hover
It provides a title or description for the table, aiding accessibility
It defines the table's column headers
correct: It provides a title or description for the table, aiding accessibility

question: Which form element attribute prevents a user from submitting a form if the field is empty, without using JavaScript?
correct: required

question: What is the difference between <input type='submit'> and <button type='submit'>?
options: They are functionally identical with no differences
<button> can contain HTML content (icons, bold text) while <input> only shows plain text
<input type='submit'> works without a form; <button> requires a form parent
<button> always uses GET method; <input type='submit'> uses POST
correct: <button> can contain HTML content (icons, bold text) while <input> only shows plain text

question: In HTML, what is the correct use of the <fieldset> and <legend> elements?
options: <fieldset> groups related form controls; <legend> provides a caption for the group
<fieldset> adds a border to inputs; <legend> labels individual inputs
<legend> groups form elements; <fieldset> labels the group
<fieldset> is used for layout; <legend> is its heading tag
correct: <fieldset> groups related form controls; <legend> provides a caption for the group

question: What does the 'for' attribute in a <label> element do?
options: It iterates over form inputs like a for loop
It applies a CSS class to the input it references
It programmatically associates the label with the input whose id matches the 'for' value
It sets the default value for the associated input
correct: It programmatically associates the label with the input whose id matches the 'for' value

question: What is the semantic difference between <strong> and <b>?
options: Both are identical; <b> is just an older version of <strong>
<strong> indicates strong importance; <b> is for stylistic bold with no implied importance
<b> indicates strong importance; <strong> is for stylistic bold
correct: <strong> indicates strong importance; <b> is for stylistic bold with no implied importance

question: Which HTML element is used to define an abbreviation and provide its full expansion?
correct: <abbr>

question: What is the correct use of the HTML <address> element?
options: It is used for any mailing address on the page
It provides contact information for its nearest <article> or <body> ancestor
It is only for email addresses
It creates a clickable address that opens Google Maps
correct: It provides contact information for its nearest <article> or <body> ancestor

question: The HTML <time> element with the attribute datetime='2025-08-15' is best described as:
options: Displaying a countdown timer on the page
Providing a machine-readable date to improve SEO and accessibility
Automatically formatting the visible date based on user's locale
Setting a deadline for form submission
correct: Providing a machine-readable date to improve SEO and accessibility

question: What is the purpose of the <aside> element in HTML5?
options: It must always appear in a sidebar column of a page layout
It represents content tangentially related to the content around it, like a sidebar or callout
It is a non-semantic container identical to <div>
It is used for hidden content not visible to the user
correct: It represents content tangentially related to the content around it, like a sidebar or callout

question: Which heading level creates the largest text by default in HTML?
options: All headings are the same size by default

question: In CSS, which statement correctly describes 'inheritance'?
options: All CSS properties are automatically passed from parent to child elements
Only certain properties like font-size and color are inherited by child elements by default
Inherited styles always override directly applied styles
Only block-level elements can inherit styles
correct: Only certain properties like font-size and color are inherited by child elements by default

question: What is the difference between inline styles, internal styles, and external stylesheets in terms of maintainability?
options: Inline styles are the most maintainable because they are closest to the HTML
External stylesheets are most maintainable as styles are centralized and reusable across pages
Internal styles are most maintainable because they avoid extra HTTP requests
correct: External stylesheets are most maintainable as styles are centralized and reusable across pages

question:
Given this CSS: body { font-size: 16px; } p { font-size: 1.5em; } span { font-size: 1.5em; } — if a is inside a

, what is the computed font size of the span?
correct: 36px

question: What unit should you use if you want an element's font-size to always be relative to the root element's font-size?
correct: rem

question: A CSS rule states: div { width: 50%; }. What is this 50% relative to?
options: The width of the div's containing (parent) block
correct: The width of the div's containing (parent) block

question:
Which CSS selector targets only

elements that are direct children of a

?
correct: div > p

question: What does the CSS adjacent sibling combinator (+) do in the rule: h2 + p { color: red; }?
options:
Styles all

elements that come after any

Styles the first

element that immediately follows an

at the same level
correct:
Styles the first

element that immediately follows an

at the same level

question: Which CSS rule correctly groups selectors to apply the same style to h1, h2, and h3?
correct: h1, h2, h3 { color: blue; }

question: What is the CSS specificity value (inline, IDs, classes, elements) for the selector: #header .nav > li:hover?
If two CSS rules have the same specificity, which one takes precedence?
options: The first rule declared in the stylesheet
The rule defined in the external stylesheet
The last rule declared in the stylesheet
correct: The last rule declared in the stylesheet

question: What does !important do in CSS, and why should its use be minimized?
options: It increases the selector's specificity by one point; it is fine to use frequently
It overrides all other declarations regardless of specificity, making the cascade hard to manage and debug
It only applies to inherited properties and has no effect on directly set properties
It marks a property as required; the browser throws an error if the property is missing
correct: It overrides all other declarations regardless of specificity, making the cascade hard to manage and debug

question: In the CSS box model, what does the 'padding' property affect?
options: The space outside the border, between the element and its neighbors
The space between the content and the border of the element
The space between the element's text and the viewport edge
correct: The space between the content and the border of the element

question: With box-sizing: border-box applied, if a div has width: 300px, padding: 20px, and border: 5px, what is the actual width of the content area?
correct: 250px

question: What CSS border shorthand correctly sets a 2px solid red border?
correct: border: 2px solid red;

question: Which CSS property controls whether a background image repeats, and what value prevents repetition entirely?
correct: background-repeat: no-repeat;

question: What is the difference between background-size: cover and background-size: contain?
options: cover scales the image to fit entirely within the element; contain scales it to fill the element completely
cover scales the image to fill the element completely (may crop); contain scales it to fit entirely without cropping
cover is for block elements; contain is for inline elements
correct: cover scales the image to fill the element completely (may crop); contain scales it to fit entirely without cropping

question: What happens to an element when you apply float: left to it?
options: It is removed from the normal document flow and positioned to the left, allowing text/elements to wrap around it
It is absolutely positioned at the left edge of the viewport
It becomes an inline element aligned to the left
It is fixed to the left side of the screen when scrolling
correct: It is removed from the normal document flow and positioned to the left, allowing text/elements to wrap around it

question: What does the CSS clear: both property do when applied to an element?
options: Removes all styles from the element
Prevents the element from being floated in any direction
Ensures the element drops below any preceding floated elements on both sides
Clears the element's padding and margin
correct: Ensures the element drops below any preceding floated elements on both sides

question: What is a 'clearfix' hack and why is it used?
options: A CSS reset that removes browser default styles
A technique to force a parent container to expand and contain its floated children
A method to prevent CSS transitions from running
A way to clear the browser cache with CSS
correct: A technique to force a parent container to expand and contain its floated children

question: What is the default value of the CSS position property?
correct: static

question: What is the key difference between position: absolute and position: fixed?
options: absolute is positioned relative to its nearest positioned ancestor; fixed is positioned relative to the viewport and does not scroll
fixed is positioned relative to its parent; absolute is relative to the viewport
absolute elements are removed from flow; fixed elements are not
They behave identically in all cases
correct: absolute is positioned relative to its nearest positioned ancestor; fixed is positioned relative to the viewport and does not scroll

question: An element has position: absolute. What happens if none of its ancestor elements have a position other than static?
options: The browser throws a CSS error
The element is positioned relative to the <body> element
The element is positioned relative to the initial containing block (typically the viewport)
The element reverts to position: relative
correct: The element is positioned relative to the initial containing block (typically the viewport)

question: What does the z-index property control, and what is required for it to take effect?
options: It controls element zoom level and always applies to any element
It controls stacking order on the z-axis and requires the element to have a position value other than static
It controls opacity layering and requires display: block
It sets the rendering depth for 3D transforms and only works with perspective
correct: It controls stacking order on the z-axis and requires the element to have a position value other than static

question: What is the CSS display difference between a <div> and a <span> by default?
correct: <div> is block-level; <span> is inline

question: What does display: inline-block allow you to do that display: inline does not?
options: Apply width, height, and vertical margin/padding to the element while keeping it in the text flow
Float the element to the left or right
correct: Apply width, height, and vertical margin/padding to the element while keeping it in the text flow

question: Which CSS property and value hides an element but still occupies its space in the layout?
correct: Both B and C

question: What is the correct CSS to style an anchor link that has already been visited?
correct: a:visited { color: purple; }

question: What is the correct order for link pseudo-classes to avoid specificity conflicts? (mnemonic: LoVe HAte or LoVe-Fear-Hate)
correct: :link, :visited, :hover, :active

question: How do you style a form input to change its border color to blue when it receives focus?
correct: input:focus { border-color: blue; }

question: What does the CSS rule input[type='text'] { ... } demonstrate?
options: An attribute selector targeting inputs with a specific type value
correct: An attribute selector targeting inputs with a specific type value

question: What is the CSS descendant selector, and how does it differ from a child selector?
options: A descendant selector (space) matches any nested level; a child selector (>) matches only direct children
A descendant selector (>) matches only direct children; a child selector (space) matches any nested level
They are identical but use different syntax
A descendant selector only works on <div> elements
correct: A descendant selector (space) matches any nested level; a child selector (>) matches only direct children

question: Which CSS pseudo-element adds content before an element without modifying the HTML?
correct: Both ::before and :before (both syntaxes are valid)

question: Given: .box::after { content: ''; display: block; clear: both; } — what pattern does this represent?
options: A clearfix pattern using a pseudo-element to contain floats
correct: A clearfix pattern using a pseudo-element to contain floats

question: What is the CSS :nth-child() pseudo-class? Which selector targets every even-numbered list item?
correct: li:nth-child(even) { }

question: What is the difference between :nth-child(n) and :nth-of-type(n)?
options: :nth-child(n) counts all sibling elements; :nth-of-type(n) counts only siblings of the same tag type
:nth-of-type(n) counts all siblings; :nth-child(n) counts only matching tag types
:nth-child(n) applies only to lists; :nth-of-type(n) applies to all elements
correct: :nth-child(n) counts all sibling elements; :nth-of-type(n) counts only siblings of the same tag type

question: What value of the CSS font-weight property renders text as bold?
correct: font-weight: 600; (or higher, commonly 700)

question: What does the CSS shorthand font: italic bold 16px/1.5 'Arial', sans-serif declare?
options: font-style, font-weight, font-size, letter-spacing, and font-family
font-style, font-weight, font-size, line-height, and font-family
font-style, font-weight, font-size, word-spacing, and font-family
Only font-size and font-family; other values are invalid in shorthand
correct: font-style, font-weight, font-size, line-height, and font-family

question: What is the purpose of a CSS reset stylesheet (like Normalize.css)?
options: It removes all default browser stylesheets and starts from zero styling
It normalizes browser inconsistencies in default styles while preserving useful defaults
It is the same as a CSS reset; Normalize.css and CSS reset are interchangeable
correct: It normalizes browser inconsistencies in default styles while preserving useful defaults

question: How do you correctly import an external CSS file within another CSS file?
correct: @import url('other.css');

question: Which CSS property controls the space between lines of text within a paragraph?
correct: line-height

question: What does the CSS property overflow: hidden do to an element?
options: Clips any content that extends beyond the element's bounds, hiding the overflow
Adds a scrollbar automatically when content overflows
Prevents child elements from being positioned outside the parent
correct: Clips any content that extends beyond the element's bounds, hiding the overflow

question: What CSS technique would you use to vertically and horizontally center a div of unknown dimensions inside its parent?
options: display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center; on the parent
correct: display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center; on the parent

question: What will this CSS code produce? div { width: 100px; margin: 0 auto; }
options: The div will be pinned to the top of the page
The div will be horizontally centered within its block-level parent
The div will have zero margin on all sides
The div will be vertically centered within its parent
correct: The div will be horizontally centered within its block-level parent

question: In CSS, what is 'specificity' and how is it calculated?
options: Specificity is the order of rules; later rules win
Specificity is a weight system: inline styles > IDs > classes/attributes/pseudo-classes > elements/pseudo-elements
Specificity is determined by the file size of the stylesheet
Specificity applies only to inherited properties
correct: Specificity is a weight system: inline styles > IDs > classes/attributes/pseudo-classes > elements/pseudo-elements

question: Consider: .nav li a { color: blue; } and #main a { color: red; }. Both apply to an <a> inside an <li> inside .nav and #main. Which color wins?
options: blue, because .nav li a has more selectors
red, because the ID selector (#main) has higher specificity than a class selector
blue, because class selectors always beat ID selectors
The last declared rule wins regardless of specificity
correct: red, because the ID selector (#main) has higher specificity than a class selector

question: What is the HTML <meta charset='UTF-8'> declaration used for?
options: It sets the language of the document
It specifies the character encoding so the browser renders text correctly
It is required for SEO and search engine ranking
It links the HTML page to a CSS stylesheet
correct: It specifies the character encoding so the browser renders text correctly

question: What is the role of the <meta name='viewport' content='width=device-width, initial-scale=1'> tag?
options: It enables GPU-accelerated rendering on mobile
It tells the browser to match the page width to the device screen width at 1:1 scale, essential for responsive design
It sets the default zoom level for desktop browsers
It prevents users from zooming into the page
correct: It tells the browser to match the page width to the device screen width at 1:1 scale, essential for responsive design

question: Which HTML element goes inside <head> and does NOT directly render visible content?
correct: <meta>

question: What is the correct way to add a comment in HTML?
correct: <!-- This is a comment -->

question: What is the correct CSS comment syntax?
correct: /* This is a comment */

question: Which HTML5 input type provides a date picker in most modern browsers?
correct: <input type='date'>

question: What is the use of the <datalist> element in HTML5 forms?
options: It creates a multi-select dropdown list
It provides a list of predefined options for autocomplete suggestions on an input
It replaces the <select> element with a searchable list
It is used to list data tables inside a form
correct: It provides a list of predefined options for autocomplete suggestions on an input

question: What is the default behavior of the CSS margin property on block-level elements when set to margin: 0 auto?
options: It removes all margins and applies 'auto' as a fallback
It centers the element horizontally by distributing available horizontal space equally on both sides
It centers the element both horizontally and vertically
It is invalid CSS and will be ignored by browsers
correct: It centers the element horizontally by distributing available horizontal space equally on both sides

question: What is 'margin collapsing' in CSS?
options: When margins from two adjacent block elements combine into a single margin equal to the larger of the two
When a margin is set to 0 and collapses to a negative value
When margins are removed from floated elements
When margin and padding conflict and the browser ignores the margin
correct: When margins from two adjacent block elements combine into a single margin equal to the larger of the two

question: Which CSS rule makes the header sticky so it stays at the top of the viewport when the user scrolls?
options: Both A and B achieve the same result
correct: header { position: sticky; top: 0; }

question: Which CSS property controls the stacking context and visual layering of overlapping elements?
correct: z-index

question: How do you use CSS to apply styles exclusively when the viewport width is 768px or less?
correct: @media screen and (max-width: 768px) { }

question: What does the HTML attribute data-* allow developers to do?
options: Store data in an external database linked to the HTML element
Embed custom private data attributes in HTML elements for use by scripts
Declare the data type of an input field
correct: Embed custom private data attributes in HTML elements for use by scripts

question: What is the CSS :not() pseudo-class used for? Provide an example.
options: It targets elements with a specific property: div:not(color) { }
It selects elements that do NOT match the specified selector: li:not(.active) { } selects all li without class 'active'
It negates all styles applied to an element
It is used to exclude elements from inheritance
correct: It selects elements that do NOT match the specified selector: li:not(.active) { } selects all li without class 'active'

question: What does the CSS property white-space: nowrap do?
options: Collapses multiple white spaces into one
Prevents text from wrapping to the next line
Adds white space around an element
correct: Prevents text from wrapping to the next line

question: What is the correct HTML to make an image a hyperlink?
Which CSS property is used to change the style of list item markers (bullets/numbers)?
correct: list-style-type

question: Which CSS value for list-style-type would use uppercase Roman numerals?
correct: upper-roman

question: What is wrong with this CSS code: #myDiv { colour: red; font-size: 14; }?
options: 'colour' is not a valid CSS property (should be 'color'), and font-size needs a unit (e.g., 14px)
ID selectors cannot have more than one property
The semicolon after the last property is incorrect
correct: 'colour' is not a valid CSS property (should be 'color'), and font-size needs a unit (e.g., 14px)

question: In CSS, how do you target an element with BOTH class 'active' and class 'highlight'?
correct: .active.highlight { }

question: What is the output of this CSS rule applied to a paragraph: p { text-transform: capitalize; } with content 'hello world'?
correct: Hello World

question: Which CSS property would you use to prevent a table's borders from doubling up between adjacent cells?
correct: border-collapse: collapse;

question: What is the purpose of the <thead>, <tbody>, and <tfoot> elements in HTML tables?
options: They are decorative; they don't affect table structure or accessibility
They semantically group table rows for better accessibility, styling control, and browser rendering
They are required for every HTML table or the table will not render
They replace <tr> and <td> in modern HTML5
correct: They semantically group table rows for better accessibility, styling control, and browser rendering

question: What is the difference between the HTML attributes 'id' and 'class'?
options: Both can be applied to multiple elements; the difference is only stylistic
An id must be unique per page; a class can be applied to multiple elements
A class must be unique per page; an id can be applied to multiple elements
id is for CSS only; class is for JavaScript only
correct: An id must be unique per page; a class can be applied to multiple elements

question: What does the rel='noopener noreferrer' attribute do when used with target='_blank' on a link?
options: It prevents the opened page from loading external resources
It prevents the new tab from accessing the original page via window.opener and hides the referrer header, improving security
It forces the link to open in an iframe
It blocks the browser's back button for the new tab
correct: It prevents the new tab from accessing the original page via window.opener and hides the referrer header, improving security

question: Which HTML element is used to embed a scalable vector graphic inline in HTML?
correct: <svg>

question: What is the CSS object-fit property used for?
options: It controls how a replaced element (like or
It sets how CSS objects inherit properties
It defines the stacking order of overlapping elements
It applies a 3D transform to elements
correct: It controls how a replaced element (like or

question: What does the following code do: input:checked + label { font-weight: bold; }?
options: Makes the label bold when the input receives focus
Makes the label bold that immediately follows a checked input (works with checkboxes/radio buttons)
Targets all labels that are siblings of any input
It is invalid CSS because input and label cannot be combined
correct: Makes the label bold that immediately follows a checked input (works with checkboxes/radio buttons)

question: What CSS property controls the rendering direction of text (e.g., left-to-right or right-to-left)?
correct: direction

question: Which of the following correctly applies multiple CSS classes to a single HTML element?
correct: <div class='box red'>

question: What will the following CSS do: a { text-decoration: none; } a:hover { text-decoration: underline; }?
options: All links will always be underlined
Links will show no underline by default and show an underline only when hovered
Links will have an underline that disappears on hover
No underline will ever show because text-decoration: none overrides hover
correct: Links will show no underline by default and show an underline only when hover

question: What is the HTML5 <figure> element typically paired with, and what is its purpose?
options: ; to add a border around images
<figcaption>; to associate a caption with self-contained content like images, charts, or code snippets
<caption>; to provide a title for a table
<footer>; to add a footnote to a section
correct: <figcaption>; to associate a caption with self-contained content like images, charts, or code snippets

question: What is the CSS word-break: break-all property used for?
options: It splits words into separate letters
It forces line breaks between words only
It allows long words or strings to be broken and wrapped at any character to prevent overflow
correct: It allows long words or strings to be broken and wrapped at any character to prevent overflow

question: Which CSS property would you use to add space between the content of a table cell and its border?
correct: padding

question: In a CSS hero section, what combination of properties would make a full-viewport-height section with a centered background image that always covers the section?
correct: height: 100vh; background-size: cover; background-position: center;

question: What is the correct way to link a favicon (browser tab icon) in HTML?
correct: <link rel='icon' href='favicon.ico' type='image/x-icon'>

question: What is the purpose of the HTML <base> element?
options: It defines the base font size for all text on the page
It specifies the base URL for all relative URLs in the document and optionally a default target
It sets the base background color of the page
It is the root container element of all HTML documents
correct: It specifies the base URL for all relative URLs in the document and optionally a default target

question: What does the CSS property cursor: pointer do when applied to a <div>?
options: Makes the div respond to click events in JavaScript
Changes the mouse cursor to a hand/pointer when hovering over the div, indicating it is clickable
Enables touch events on mobile devices
correct: Changes the mouse cursor to a hand/pointer when hovering over the div, indicating it is clickable

question: What is the difference between the HTML <header> element and the <head> element?
options: They are synonymous and can be used in place of each other
<head> contains metadata not displayed on the page; <header> is a visible semantic section for introductory content
<header> contains metadata; <head> is a visible content section
<head> appears in the <body>; <header> appears outside the <body>
correct: <head> contains metadata not displayed on the page; <header> is a visible semantic section for introductory content

question: Which of the following is a valid way to use an ID selector in CSS?
correct: #myID { color: blue; }

question: What does the HTML attribute 'tabindex' control?
options: The number of tabs displayed in a browser window
The order in which elements receive keyboard focus when the Tab key is pressed
The indentation level of the element
The z-index stacking order of overlapping elements
correct: The order in which elements receive keyboard focus when the Tab key is pressed

question:
What will happen in this HTML:

Line 1
Line 2

?
options: A new paragraph will begin after 'Line 1'
A line break will be inserted inside the paragraph, placing 'Line 2' on the next line

The
will be treated as invisible and both lines appear on the same line
The browser will render an error symbol
correct: A line break will be inserted inside the paragraph, placing 'Line 2' on the next line

question:
What is the output of this HTML:

Price: <b>100</b>

?
options: Price: 100 (rendered as literal text, not bold)
correct: Price: 100 (rendered as literal text, not bold)

question: Which CSS property controls the opacity of an element, and what is the range of valid values?
options: opacity: 0 to 1, where 0 is fully transparent and 1 is fully opaque
correct: opacity: 0 to 1, where 0 is fully transparent and 1 is fully opaque

question: What is a CSS 'stacking context' and what creates one?
options: A named CSS layer created with @layer that controls cascade order
A three-dimensional rendering context created whenever an element has position: static
A self-contained layering environment created by certain CSS properties (like position + z-index, opacity < 1, transform, filter), within which z-index values are isolated
An ordering system that is only created by the root HTML element
correct: A self-contained layering environment created by certain CSS properties (like position + z-index, opacity < 1, transform, filter), within which z-index values are isolated

question: A <nav> element is placed inside a <footer>. Is this valid HTML5?
options: No, <nav> can only appear once per page inside <header>
Yes, <nav> can be used inside <footer> for secondary navigation such as site links
Yes, but only if the <nav> contains at least 3 links
correct: Yes, <nav> can be used inside <footer> for secondary navigation such as site links

question: What is a CSS 'box shadow' and which property applies it? Give an example.
options: shadow-box: 2px 2px 5px black; — adds a drop shadow
box-shadow: 2px 2px 5px black; — offsets a shadow horizontally, vertically, with blur and color
drop-shadow: 2px 2px 5px black; — applies a filter-based shadow
element-shadow: offset-x offset-y blur color; — applies to block elements only
correct: box-shadow: 2px 2px 5px black; — offsets a shadow horizontally, vertically, with blur and color

question: In HTML forms, what is the difference between GET and POST methods?
options: GET appends data to the URL (visible, limited length); POST sends data in the request body (not visible in URL, no practical size limit)
POST appends data to the URL; GET sends data in the request body
GET is for file uploads; POST is for text inputs
GET is more secure than POST because data is encrypted
correct: GET appends data to the URL (visible, limited length); POST sends data in the request body (not visible in URL, no practical size limit)

question: What does the CSS universal selector (*) do?
options: It selects only the root element of the document
It selects every element on the page
It selects all class attributes across the page
It is a wildcard that only applies inside a specific selector
correct: It selects every element on the page

question: What is wrong with this HTML form: <form><input type='text' name='user'><input type='submit'></form> — from an accessibility standpoint?
options: The form is missing an action attribute
The text input is missing a <label> element, making it unclear to screen reader users what the field is for
The submit button must have a name attribute
The form method should be explicitly declared
correct: The text input is missing a <label> element, making it unclear to screen reader users what the field is for

question: Which HTML5 element is used to represent self-contained content that can be independently reused?
correct: <article>

question: Which element is used for marking navigation links in HTML5?
correct: <nav>

question: Which of the following is correct about the difference between <section> and <div>?
correct: <section> is semantic; <div> is non-semantic

question: Which is NOT a valid HTML5 element?
correct: <sidebar>

question: Which CSS3 feature allows you to create rounded corners?
correct: border-radius

question: Which of the following creates a linear gradient background?
correct: linear-gradient(red, blue)

question: Which CSS3 property is used for transitions?
correct: transition

question: What will this code do: p::first-line { font-weight: bold; }?
correct: Makes first line bold

question: Which CSS3 layout module allows flexible box-based design?
correct: Flexbox

question: What does this rule do: div { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 2fr; }?
correct: Creates 2 columns, 1:2 ratio

question: Which CSS3 property is used to apply a shadow to text?
correct: text-shadow

question: What does the following CSS code achieve: div:hover { transform: rotate(15deg); }?
correct: Rotates the div when hovered

question: The '<picture>' element is used primarily for:
options: Providing responsive images with multiple sources
Creating image maps with clickable areas
Defining a container for SVG graphics
correct: Providing responsive images with multiple sources

question: For the code '<label for='email-address'>Email:</label><input type='email' id='email-address' name='email'>', which attribute must be unique on the page?
correct: The 'id' attribute in the '<input>' tag

question: What is the key semantic purpose of the '<article>' element compared to '<section>'?
options: '<article>' is for standalone, distributable content, while '<section>' is for thematic grouping.
'<article>' must always be nested inside a '<section>'.
'<section>' is for standalone content, while '<article>' is for grouping.
They are semantically identical and can be used interchangeably.
correct: '<article>' is for standalone, distributable content, while '<section>' is for thematic grouping.

question: Which input type would be most appropriate for selecting a value from a range of 1 to 100?
options: Both A and B are equally appropriate.
correct: Both A and B are equally appropriate.

question: To pre-select an '<option>' in a dropdown menu when the page loads, you use the attribute:
correct: selected='selected'

question: Which tag is NOT a valid HTML5 semantic tag?
correct: <frame>

question: The 'async' attribute in a '<script>' tag means:
options: The script will be executed synchronously in the order it appears.
The script will be downloaded asynchronously and executed immediately, pausing the parser.
The script will be executed after the 'DOMContentLoaded' event.
The script will only run if the user interacts with the page.
correct: The script will be downloaded asynchronously and executed immediately, pausing the parser.

question: To create a required text input field that must be filled out, you use:
correct: <input type='text' required>

question: The 'sandbox' attribute in an '<iframe>' is used to:
options: Improve the loading performance of the iframe.
Create a restricted environment for the content, enhancing security.
Allow the iframe content to access the parent page's DOM.
Enable seamless rendering of the iframe border.
correct: Create a restricted environment for the content, enhancing security.

question: Which code snippet correctly links a document-level CSS style sheet?
correct: <link href='styles.css' rel='stylesheet'>

question: Which selector has the highest specificity?
correct: #nav .list li a:hover

question: Which code will create a 20px blur effect and move a div 30px to the right?
correct: filter: blur(20px); transform: translateX(30px);

question:
To style every '

' element that is the first child of its parent, you would use:
correct: All of the above
